What is the story of Oedipe and his mother ?

The story of Oedipus and his mother is a Greek tragedy that has been recounted in many variations over the years. The most famous version of the story is attributed to the playwright Sophocles, who wrote "Oedipus Rex" sometime in the early 5th century BCE.

The story begins with the King and Queen of Thebes, Laius and Jocasta, who are warned by an oracle that their son will grow up to kill his father and marry his mother. In order to avoid this fate, the couple abandon their child in the mountains, hoping that he will die.

However, the child, Oedipus, is rescued by a shepherd and raised in a neighboring kingdom. When he grows up, Oedipus is also visited by an oracle and told the same prophecy. Determined to avoid his fate, Oedipus decides to leave his adopted parents and set out on his own.

On his journey, Oedipus unknowingly kills his biological father Laius and marries his mother Jocasta, fulfilling the prophecy. When Oedipus discovers the truth about his ancestry, he is horrified and blinds himself.

The story of Oedipus and his mother is a cautionary tale about the dangers of trying to escape one's fate, as well as the cyclical nature of tragedy in Greek mythology.

Does Oedipus have children with his mother ?

In the classic Greek myth, Oedipus marries his mother Jocasta and they have four children together, two sons named Eteocles and Polynices, and two daughters named Antigone and Ismene. However, when Oedipus learns the truth about his relationship with Jocasta, he is horrified and blinds himself. Jocasta, meanwhile, is so distraught that she takes her own life, leaving their children orphaned.

Once upon a time, there was a kingdom called Thebes. The King and Queen, Laius and Jocasta, were informed by an oracle that their son would grow up to kill his father and marry his mother. Terrified, they decided to abandon their son, Oedipus, in the mountains.

However, as fate would have it, a kind shepherd finds the baby boy and takes him to another kingdom to be raised. There, Oedipus grows up to become a brave and noble young man.

Years later, Oedipus returns to Thebes and saves the kingdom from a terrible monster threatening their people. Impressed by his heroism, the people of Thebes ask Oedipus to become their king. Jocasta, who is unaware of Oedipus' true identity, falls in love with him and they are married with the blessing of the people.

Soon they have four children, two sons named Eteocles and Polynices, as well as two daughters named Antigone and Ismene. Oedipus and Jocasta raise their children with great care, love and affection, always trying their best to give them a happy life.

As years go by, Oedipus begins to hear rumors about his past, and with the help of a wise seer, discovers that he is Laius and Jocasta's son, and that he has fulfilled the prophecy. Overwhelmed by this revelation, Oedipus tells Jocasta everything and prepares to leave Thebes forever.

But Jocasta, who loves Oedipus deeply and cannot imagine life without him, reminds him of the love they share and of the fact that they have raised their children with great care and affection. She convinces him that they can overcome their terrible fate and bequeath their legacy to their children, who are innocent and full of promise.

Oedipus and Jocasta stay together, raise their children and rule Thebes with justice, kindness and wisdom. Their family grows into a happy and loving one, respected and admired by their people for generations to come. And though they could not change the prophecy, they conquered it with their love and devotion, and lived happily ever after.
